Title: Redox buffering and H2O2 orchestrate the vegetative development of Marchantia polymorpha

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Class: Reactive oxygen species; antioxidant redox buffer
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Balances cell proliferation and differentiation during vegetative development
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Meristematic region and differentiated thallus tissues
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Exogenous MpCLE2 peptide expands the meristematic region and increases H2O2 levels and GSH oxidation in the enlarged meristem and differentiated tissues
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Exogenous peptide treatment; morphology analysis; roGFP2-hGrx1 imaging; HyPer7 imaging
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MpCLE2 peptide treatment enlarged the meristematic region in BoGa gemmae. 2. Expanded meristems showed increased GSH oxidation and elevated H2O2 sensor ratios. 3. Multiple elongated oxidized H2O2 zones formed in the enlarged meristem, linking stem-cell-promoting signaling to redox remodeling.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Meristem overproliferation accompanied by elevated oxidative status
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper experimentally used the peptide MpCLE2p rather than direct gene manipulation. Included as a core common gene because MpCLE2 signaling was a primary experimental driver of redox/metabolic change in Results.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating CLE-mediated meristem signaling may tune growth via redox balance, but may also provoke overproliferation and oxidative imbalance.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Redox buffering, ROS homeostasis, support of meristem activity and vegetative growth
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Meristematic region and differentiated thallus tissues
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reduced MpGSH1 expression lowers total GSH content, increases oxidation of the GSH redox sensor, weakens/abolishes the meristem-to-thallus GSH redox gradient under stronger depletion, and causes growth arrest when GSH is further reduced by BSO
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Artificial miRNA knockdown; qRT-PCR; DTNB recycling assay; roGFP2-hGrx1 sensor imaging; BSO treatment; growth measurements
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MpGSH1 is the sole tested GSH biosynthetic gene and is required to sustain glutathione levels in Marchantia polymorpha. 2. Knockdown to 5-7% transcript reduced total GSH to 11-27% of WT and increased tissue oxidation, yet plants remained viable if a residual redox gradient was maintained. 3. Further GSH depletion with BSO abolished the redox gradient and arrested gemma growth, indicating that maintenance of the GSH gradient rather than absolute redox value is crucial for development.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step supported; complete knockout likely lethal and partial depletion strongly affects GSH pool and growth under further depletion
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: MpEF1a constitutive promoter used for amiRNA construct and sensor lines
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Severe growth arrest under stronger GSH depletion; complete loss likely lethal
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The study emphasizes redox-gradient maintenance as developmentally decisive. The applied species is Marchantia polymorpha ssp. ruderalis ecotype BoGa; source and applied species are the same.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Maintaining adequate GSH biosynthesis and especially spatial redox gradients could be a target to optimize growth robustness under oxidative challenges, but strong downregulation risks lethality or growth arrest.
